prep_method: Soak in water + baking soda (5g per bowl of water) for 1–3 hours before cooking. This raises pH, softens the meat, and pulls oxidized fats and stress-related compounds from the muscle tissue. Rinse thoroughly before cooking.
cooking_method: Cook in oven in the meat's own fat. Rare to medium-rare only — never overcook. Do not high-heat fry in oil. If pan-cooking, use cast iron or stainless steel with butter, ghee, or tallow only.
avoid: Never cook in seed/vegetable oils. Never overcook (well-done creates heterocyclic amines and oxidized fat). Avoid microwave for primary cooking. Do not marinate in acidic commercial sauces containing seed oils or sugar.
timing: Any meal; post-workout second meal (2–3h after training, protein + fat with no carbs); evening meal as protein + fat base
notes: Beef, lamb, and bison are the base of the androgen diet — providing heme iron, zinc, B12, creatine, carnitine, saturated fat, and cholesterol for steroidogenesis. Grass-fed and pasture-raised preferred for higher fat-soluble vitamin content. Cook to rare/medium-rare to preserve nutrient density.

prep_method: Soak liver in cold water + baking soda (5g per bowl) for 1–3 hours before cooking to reduce the strong mineral taste and pull any residual oxidized compounds. Rinse thoroughly. Pat dry before cooking. Optionally, soak in milk (A2 dairy only if using) for 30–60 min to further mellow flavor.
cooking_method: Cook gently in butter, ghee, or tallow on medium heat. Liver should be cooked to just done — slightly pink inside. Do not overcook; overcooked liver becomes dry and loses texture. Thin slices (1cm) cook in 2–3 min per side.
avoid: Never fry in seed/vegetable oils. Never overcook to well-done — destroys sensitive B-vitamins. Do not combine with high-dose zinc supplement on same day without monitoring copper balance.
timing: 2–3 times per week, 50–100g per serving. Any meal except immediately post-workout (reserve post-workout for lean protein + starch).
notes: Most nutrient-dense food in the protocol: vitamins A, B12, folate, copper, choline — all critical for steroidogenesis, thyroid conversion, and methylation. Frequency cap: 50–100g 2–3×/week due to vitamin A content. Beef liver is the most recommended; chicken liver is also acceptable.

prep_method: No special soaking required. Rinse shell under running water before cracking if using in cooking. Pasture-raised eggs preferred for higher omega-3 and fat-soluble vitamin content.
cooking_method: Preferred methods: soft-boiled (6–7 min), poached, or fried in butter/tallow/ghee at low-medium heat (preserve yolk integrity). Scrambled in butter at low heat. For post-workout protein: egg whites only (lightly cooked) in pre-workout or post-workout protein meals. For general meals: whole eggs with yolks — yolks contain cholesterol, choline, and fat-soluble vitamins.
avoid: Do not cook eggs in seed/vegetable oils. Do not consistently consume raw egg whites — avidin in raw whites binds and depletes biotin. Raw yolks are fine. Avoid overcooked rubbery scrambled eggs (high heat denatures protein quality and destroys choline).
timing: 3–10 eggs per day as tolerated. Morning, midday, evening meals. Egg whites (cooked) as pre-workout protein source. Whole eggs as base protein in all non-post-workout meals.
notes: Whole eggs with yolks provide: cholesterol (steroid precursor), choline, fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, K, E), complete protein, and selenium. Do not discard yolks. For hair washing: 2–4 raw pasture-raised eggs — see raw egg hair washing protocol.

prep_method: Cook fresh before post-workout use. Rinse rice once before cooking. Cook per normal boil or rice cooker method.
cooking_method: Boil or steam. Short-grain or jasmine rice preferred for fastest glucose absorption post-workout. Cook fresh and eat hot — do not cool, chill, or refrigerate before the post-workout meal.
avoid: Do not use cooled or refrigerated rice in the post-workout window — cooling converts starch to resistant starch via retrogradation, significantly slowing glucose absorption and blunting the GLUT4 window. Do not add butter/oil in the first post-workout meal (fat ≤5–7g in this window). Do not use brown rice post-workout (slower, higher fiber).
timing: Primary use: post-workout meal (0–30 min after training) as 100–200g hot fresh starch + lean protein. Secondary: intra-training carb base if dextrose unavailable. Rest day carb base (reduced portion). Not for pre-workout or evening meals.
notes: Simple starch for glycogen replenishment and thyroid/insulin support. 'Hot freshly cooked potatoes are the fastest; hot rice is very close.' Jasmine/short-grain rice absorbs fastest. Basmati absorbs slower. White rice is approved contextual — timing matters.

prep_method: Peel potatoes before cooking. Peeled potatoes remove most lectins and oxalates from the skin. Freshly peeled and freshly cooked — not stored/chilled after cooking.
cooking_method: For post-workout: peel, boil until soft, mash hot — this is the fastest-absorbing post-workout starch option. Or bake whole in oven (with skin for baking; peel before eating). Do not add butter/cream/oil in the post-workout meal (fat ≤5–7g). For general meals: roast in tallow or bake; can add animal fat outside the post-workout window.
avoid: Do not use cooled/refrigerated potatoes in the post-workout window (retrogradation creates resistant starch → much slower). Do not use sweet potatoes post-workout (slower and higher oxalate content). Do not use potato chips or any potato product fried in seed oils.
timing: Primary use: post-workout meal (0–30 min after training) as 100–200g hot freshly cooked/mashed starch + lean protein. General meals: any time as carb base. Reduce portion on rest days (~25% reduction in total carbs).
notes: Potatoes are one of two approved carb exceptions in the vegetable category. Hot freshly mashed potatoes are described as the fastest post-workout starch option. Always freshly cooked — never chilled or pre-cooked. Skin washing: soak 15–60 min in water with natural soap or baking soda/vinegar if consumed.

prep_method: PREP REQUIRED — do not eat oats plain or unfermented. Step 1: Soak oats in water for several hours or overnight. Step 2: Discard all soaking water completely. Step 3: Add raw A2 dairy kefir or plain A2 yogurt to the oats. Step 4: Ferment at room temperature for ~24 hours. The fermentation degrades phytic acid (which blocks zinc, magnesium, and iron absorption), reduces avenin (an oat prolamin that irritates the gut), ferments the starch (flattens insulin spike), and improves mineral bioavailability.
cooking_method: After 24h fermentation: consume cold as-is (kefir/yogurt fermented) or warm gently on low heat. Do not boil the fermented oats at high heat — destroys the beneficial fermentation. Add no seed oils, no sugar, no fruit. Can add raw honey (micro-dose only, pre-bed timing) if desired.
avoid: Never eat oats plain, unsoaked, or unfermented — the phytic acid content makes them a mineral blocker in their raw/basic state. Do not use commercial flavored oatmeal packets (contain sugar, seed oils). Do not use instant oats (highly processed). Do not eat immediately post-workout — oats are the slowest starch and not suitable for the GLUT4 window.
timing: General meals only — not post-workout. Best used as a carb base for morning or rest-day meals after fermentation. Not a daily default; use white rice and potatoes as primary starch sources.
notes: Oats are classified as PREP REQUIRED — not generically approved. The preparation protocol described by the author is specific: soak, discard water, ferment in A2 kefir/yogurt 24h. Without this prep, oats are borderline problematic for the KB's mineral absorption and gut health framework.

prep_method: Use grass-fed or pasture-raised bones (beef marrow bones, knuckles, feet preferred for gelatin content). Optionally roast bones at 400°F/200°C for 30–40 min before simmering to deepen flavor. Cover with filtered water.
cooking_method: Simmer on very low heat for 12–24 hours (beef bones) or 4–8 hours (chicken/fish bones). Add a splash of apple cider vinegar at the start to help extract minerals from bones. Skim foam in the first hour. Strain and store. Consume 1–2 cups per day or throughout the week.
avoid: Do not use high heat or rapid boiling — degrades collagen into gelatin less effectively. Do not use bones from factory-farmed animals if possible (higher toxin accumulation in bone marrow). Do not add starches or seed oils.
timing: 1–2 cups daily or several times per week. Any time of day. Can be used as a cooking base or drunk warm as a standalone drink.
notes: Provides collagen precursors (glycine, proline, hydroxyproline), gelatin, minerals (calcium, phosphorus, magnesium), and joint-supporting compounds. Supports gut lining integrity. Weekly routine includes bone broth as a regular anchor food.

prep_method: Source matters: use A2 milk (from breeds like Guernsey, Jersey, or marketed A2 products), goat milk/kefir/yogurt, or sheep dairy. Raw dairy from trusted sources is preferred by the author. Do not use standard supermarket dairy from conventional grain-fed A1 cows.
cooking_method: Use as-is (raw kefir/yogurt), or warm gently (do not boil raw dairy — destroys enzymes and live cultures). Use A2 kefir or raw yogurt as the fermentation medium for oats. Consume later in the day (not in the first post-workout meal window where fat must be kept ≤5–7g).
avoid: Avoid all standard supermarket dairy from conventional grain-fed cows (A1 beta-casein → BCM-7 peptide → gut irritation, insulin spike, estrogenic signaling). Avoid flavored/sweetened dairy products (contain sugar and seed oils).
timing: Later in the day (not in post-workout first meal window). With oat fermentation protocol (as the kefir/yogurt fermenting medium). As a protein and fat source in evening meals if tolerated.
notes: A2 and goat/sheep dairy are context-approved — source and type matter significantly. Raw fermented forms (kefir, yogurt) provide additional probiotic benefit. Some individuals do not tolerate any dairy — if GI symptoms persist after 2–3 week trial, remove entirely.

prep_method: Fresh oysters: shuck and consume raw, or steam lightly (2–3 min). Canned oysters: rinse to reduce sodium content if needed; consume with the packing liquid (typically in water or their own juice, not vegetable oil). Mussels, clams, shrimp: steam or boil briefly.
cooking_method: Raw (freshest form for maximum nutrient density), steamed (gentle method, 2–5 min), or cooked briefly in butter on low heat. Do not fry in oil. Do not bread or batter.
avoid: Avoid oysters/shellfish fried in seed oils (e.g. fast food fried seafood). Avoid canned shellfish in vegetable/soybean oil — choose water-packed. Do not overcook (reduces zinc and selenium bioavailability).
timing: Every other day, 1 can or 6 large oysters. Any meal time. Excellent as a zinc + selenium + copper + iodine anchor for the micronutrient base.
notes: Best natural food source of zinc, copper, selenium, and iodine — all required for steroidogenesis, thyroid T4→T3 conversion, and 5AR function. Frequency: every other day. Pair with red meat and eggs for complete micronutrient coverage.

prep_method: Cook starch fresh and eat hot. Starch options: hot freshly cooked white rice (jasmine/short-grain fastest) OR freshly peeled and mashed hot potatoes. Cook protein lean: whey isolate mixed in water, egg whites lightly scrambled, or lean meat (chicken breast, white fish, lean beef). Prepare before or immediately after training so meal is ready within the 30-min window.
cooking_method: Rice: boil/steam fresh, serve hot. Potatoes: peel, boil, mash hot. Lean protein: prepare simply — no added fats, no sauces with sugar or seed oils. Meal fat target: ≤5–7g total. No fructose. No butter or oil in this meal.
avoid: No fat beyond ≤5–7g in this meal. No butter, no olive oil, no coconut oil in this window. No fructose: no fruit, no honey, no sucrose. No cooled/chilled or leftover starch. No brown rice, sweet potatoes, oats, or any slower/higher-fiber starch. No high-fat protein source (no ribeye, no whole eggs — use lean protein only).
timing: Within 30 minutes of last training set. This is the most time-critical meal in the protocol — GLUT4 starts closing after ~30–60 min. Delay costs nutrient uptake efficiency.
notes: This is the convergence window: GLUT4, insulin sensitivity, AMPK, and mTOR all peak simultaneously. 'Most guides say eat carbs and protein after training. That is like saying put fuel in a car.' The detail matters: hot starch, lean protein, near-zero fat, no fructose, within 30 min. Supplements with this meal: R-ALA 300mg + HMB 1g + Taurine 2g + joint stack.

prep_method: Prepare a lean protein source 30–60 min before training. Options: Whey isolate 30–40g mixed in water (not milk, not juice); OR 300ml egg whites lightly scrambled (no yolks in this meal — fat must stay ≤5–10g); OR lean fish (cod, tilapia, white fish) 150–200g plain; OR lean meat 150–200g (chicken breast or very lean beef). Add a pinch of sodium (Celtic sea salt).
cooking_method: Simple preparation only. Egg whites: scramble lightly in a dry pan or with a trace of water — no butter, no oil. Lean fish: steam, poach, or pan-cook in water. Lean meat: grilled or baked with no oil. No sauces, marinades, or added fats.
avoid: Zero carbohydrates in this meal. No rice, no bread, no potato, no fruit, no oats. Fat strictly ≤5–10g — no yolks, no butter, no oils, no nuts. Low fiber. No dairy milk (carbs + fat). Pre-carbs spike insulin, shut lipolysis, and create energy mismatch before training.
timing: 30–60 minutes before training. Not earlier than 90 min before (digestion timing). Must be the last food before the intra-workout drink starts.
notes: Protein-only pre keeps fat burning active (lipolysis on), provides amino acid protection against catabolism, and creates a small insulin bump that doesn't block fat oxidation. 'This is the gold standard for hypertrophy/recomp.' For endurance events >90 min: pre-carbs are appropriate as an exception.

prep_method: Protein source: red meat (beef, lamb, bison) 150–250g OR 4–6 whole eggs. Fat source: cook in and/or serve with animal fat — butter, ghee, tallow, or the fat naturally present in the meat. No carbohydrates in this meal. Prepare meat per standard protocol: soak in water + baking soda 1–3h; rinse; cook in oven in own fat to rare/medium-rare.
cooking_method: Beef/lamb: oven-cook in own fat, rare to medium-rare. Eggs: fried or scrambled in butter/tallow/ghee. No carb-containing sides. No starchy vegetables, no rice, no potato in this meal.
avoid: No carbohydrates. No sugary sauces or marinades. No seed oils. No processed meats with fillers (sausages containing soy, grain fillers, or seed oil). Do not eat this meal within 2–3 hours of sleep (last large meal should be 2–3h before bed).
timing: 2–3 hours post-workout (second post-workout meal) — once GLUT4 tail has faded and no more benefit from carbs. OR as an evening meal on rest days. Finish at least 2–3 hours before sleep.
notes: This meal activates mTOR a second time via protein alone (no insulin spike needed). Protein digests slowly overnight, providing a sustained amino acid supply. Animal fat in this meal supports steroidogenesis. This is the 'fat comes back' meal after the lean post-workout first window.
